coredata.dc:title: The investigation of the interaction between fluorescent carbon dots labeling silk fibroin using a fluorescence microscope–surface plasmon resonance system
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Hongxin
Zhao, Yi
Zhang, Xiuqin
Ma, Haitao
Zhang, Hongyan
coredata.dc:description: The use of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as highly precise biolabeling probes has been widespread in the fields of live cell imaging and protein labeling due to their small size and excellent photoluminescence ability to accurately target specific molecules with surface chemical properties. However, there was a lack of research on the interaction between CDs and labeled molecules. In this work, we presented a novel investigation strategy, the fluorescence microscopy-surface plasmon resonance (FM-SPR) system, which combined the use of fluorescence microscopy and wavelength modulation surface plasmon resonance to study the interaction between CDs and labeled molecules in real-time. Using this system, simultaneously recorded the SPR signals and the fluorescence images on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip. We observed the dynamic curve and fluorescence images of the interaction between green emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and silk fibroin (SF) in real-time. The kinetic parameters, the quantitative analysis, and the investigation of the binding could be achieved. The results showed a strong linear relationship between the change in SPR signals and the concentration of N-CDs, with a linear coefficient of 0.99913. The linear detection range was 2.5 μg/mL–100 μg/mL, and the real lowest detection limit reached 0.5 μg/mL. Additionally, the green fluorescence points in the imaging region on the FM-SPR sensor chip increased with the concentration of N-CDs, which was consistent with the change in SPR signals. Using this system we also acquired the association rate and dissociation rate of N-CDs to SF which were 2.65 × 10−5/s and 1.52 × 10−5/s, respectively. This demonstrated the effectiveness of our method in quantitatively analyzing SF labeled with N-CDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots labeling probe
FM-SPR
Kinetic processes
Real-time
Fluorescence image

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of FM-SPR sensor chip substrate
Functionalization of FM-SPR sensor chip surface
Binding process of N-CDs and SF
Real-time monitoring of FM-SPR sensor chip surface modification processes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Plan of China ( 2022YFB3805804 , 2022YFB3805802 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Beijing (Grant No. 4182069 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61378044 , 60978034 ), the Classified Development of Municipal Colleges and Universities-the Project of Constructing the Emerging Interdisciplinary Platform Based on \u201CClothing Science\u201D of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology (Grant No. 11000024T000003073871 ) and the Graduate Student Research and Innovation Program of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology (Grant No. X2023-045 ).

This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Plan of China (Grant No. 2022YFB3805804, 2022YFB3805802), the Natural Science Foundation of Beijing (Grant No. 4182069), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61378044, 60978034), the Classified Development of Municipal Colleges and Universities-the Project of Constructing the Emerging Interdisciplinary Platform Based on \u201CClothing Science\u201D of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology (Grant No. 11000024T000003073871) and the Graduate Student Research and Innovation Program of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology (Grant No. X2023-045).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the FM-SPR system, with key components labeled in the figure.
Schematic diagram of the surface modification steps of FM-SPR sensor chip.
Real-time changes in the SPR signal due to chemical binding on the surface of FM-SPR sensor chip.

Changes in SPR peak positions during the surface modification of the FM-SPR sensor chip: (a) The injection order was 3-MPA, NHS/EDC, SF, and NHS/EDC in sequence, with 4 mL of PBS buffer used to wash away unbound or excess molecules after each injection of each solution. (b) The changes of the final SPR peak positions after the completion of different modification processes.

Water contact angle measurements and AFM measurements. (a, d) were the AFM characterization results and water contact angle measurements of the bare FM-SPR sensor chip, respectively; (b, e) were the AFM characterization results and water contact angle measurements of the FM-SPR sensor chip incorporating 3-MPA, respectively; (c, f) were the AFM characterization results and water contact angle measurements of the FM-SPR sensor chip incorporating 3-MPA and SF, respectively.

Real-time kinetic processes of N-CDs labeled SF: (a) real-time kinetic curves of labeled SF as the N-CDs concentration was varied from 0.5 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL, and (b) linear relationship between ΔIR/I0 and N-CDs concentration as the N-CDs concentration was varied from 0.5 μg/mL to 100 μg/mL. The RSD for each N-CDs concentration measured were 1 %, 4.4 %, 4.5 %, 1.5 %, 1 %, 2.1 %, 2.3 %, and 5 %, respectively. Error bars represented the standard deviations obtained from at least three independent measurements.

Fluorescence images were acquired from the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip at various N-CDs concentrations: 0.5 μg/mL, 1 μg/mL, 2.5 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, and 100 μg/mL, from a to h.
The association and dissociation curves of N-CDs with SF at 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL and 25 μg/mL.

head.ce:title: The investigation of the interaction between fluorescent carbon dots labeling silk fibroin using a fluorescence microscope–surface plasmon resonance system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Clothing Materials R & D and Assessment, Beijing Engineering Research Center of Textile, Nanofiber, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Clothing Materials R & D and Assessment
Beijing Engineering Research Center of Textile

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Clothing Materials R & D and Assessment, Beijing Engineering Research Center of Textile, Nanofiber, Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology, Beijing 100029, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Communication Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun Jilin 130012, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Communication Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun Jilin 130012, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The use of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) as highly precise biolabeling probes has been widespread in the fields of live cell imaging and protein labeling due to their small size and excellent photoluminescence ability to accurately target specific molecules with surface chemical properties. However, there was a lack of research on the interaction between CDs and labeled molecules. In this work, we presented a novel investigation strategy, the fluorescence microscopy-surface plasmon resonance (FM-SPR) system, which combined the use of fluorescence microscopy and wavelength modulation surface plasmon resonance to study the interaction between CDs and labeled molecules in real-time. Using this system, simultaneously recorded the SPR signals and the fluorescence images on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip. We observed the dynamic curve and fluorescence images of the interaction between green emissive nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) and silk fibroin (SF) in real-time. The kinetic parameters, the quantitative analysis, and the investigation of the binding could be achieved. The results showed a strong linear relationship between the change in SPR signals and the concentration of N-CDs, with a linear coefficient of 0.99913. The linear detection range was 2.5 μg/mL–100 μg/mL, and the real lowest detection limit reached 0.5 μg/mL. Additionally, the green fluorescence points in the imaging region on the FM-SPR sensor chip increased with the concentration of N-CDs, which was consistent with the change in SPR signals. Using this system we also acquired the association rate and dissociation rate of N-CDs to SF which were 2.65 × 10−5/s and 1.52 × 10−5/s, respectively. This demonstrated the effectiveness of our method in quantitatively analyzing SF labeled with N-CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: CDs, a class of novel nanomaterials with diameters typically less than 10 nm, held considerable potential for nanomedical applications [1,2]. CDs were recognized for their low toxicity, excellent biocompatibility, superior optical properties, ease of synthesis, and straightforward functionalization [3–7]. This spurred increasing research interest in their use in bio-imaging, fluorescent labeling, detection and adsorption of heavy metal ions, drug delivery, and preparation of strongly photoluminescent nanocomposites, and so on [8–20]. For instance, Chethana et al. synthesized orange emissive CDs and utilized N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) to activate the carboxyl groups on the CDs, allowing them to react with lysine groups present on the proteins. They successfully demonstrated the effective labeling of these proteins through UV-absorption spectroscopy, poly-acrylamide gel electrophoresis, and fluorescence correlation spectroscopy [21]. Pang et al. synthesized orange/green dual-emissive CDs (OG-CDs) using a hydrothermal method and successfully achieved the labeling of E. coli [22]. He et al. synthesized water-soluble CDs that emit blue, green and yellow fluorescence via the hydrothermal method. The results of liver frozen section experiments indicated that hepatic stellate cells could be specifically target labeled by this fluorescent probe. This provided an important technological tool for understanding liver structure and function at the cellular level, exploring pathological changes in the liver, and designing and developing drugs [23]. While numerous studies have explored the use of CDs in bio-labeling and imaging, there remained a gap in research addressing the real-time kinetic process of the reaction between the CDs and the labeled species. Furthermore, there will be a need for more quantitative analysis to better understand the relationship between CDs and the labeled species.

Surface plasmon resonance (SPR) was a physical-optical phenomenon, which was generated by the resonant oscillation of surface plasmon polaritons (SPPs) with external light waves under certain conditions. Where SPPs were electron density waves that propagate along the interface of the metal medium and the dielectric [24,25]. SPR techniques, with their capabilities for real-time, sensitive, and label-free detection, were frequently used in the investigation of interactions between biomolecules [26–31]. Despite the significant advantages of SPR in determining affinity and acquiring kinetic information, the data obtained solely through SPR method may no longer be sufficient to meet the demands of practical development. Consequently, various SPR-based coupling techniques have been proposed in recent years to augment the flow of information. For example, SPR coupled with Electrochemical Methods [32–34], SPR combined with Raman Spectroscopy [35], and SPR integrated with Mass Spectrometry [36–38] had been developed, providing insights into redox reactions, molecular structure, functional group changes. In our previous work, we developed a laser scanning confocal imaging-surface plasmon resonance (LSCI-SPR) system to monitor molecular interactions. This system has advantages in detecting small molecule interactions with high spatial resolution and sensitivity or when only the refractive index of the solution changes when large molecules interact [39,40]. The LSCI-SPR system had been applied to detect ion-ssDNA interactions [41], antibody-antigen interactions [42], large molecule-biological cell interactions [43], small molecule-biological cell interactions [44], and small molecule-gram-negative bacterial interactions [45].

In this work, we presented a simplified fluorescent microscopy-surface plasmon resonance (FM-SPR) system, developed by replacing the laser scanning confocal microscope with a basic fluorescent microscopy system. This modification retained the primary functionality of the LSCI-SPR system while significantly reducing the overall system cost. The FM-SPR system allowed for the enables real-time monitoring of the kinetic process of binding between CDs and the labeled species, facilitating quantitative analysis of this interaction. The first application of this system was the real-time simultaneous recording of kinetic curves and fluorescence images of N-CDs bound to SF. The results showed that the change in SPR peaks correlated well with the concentration of N-CDs increases, and the number of green fluorescence points within the fluorescence image also increased, mirroring the changes observed in the SPR signals. Using this system, the association rate and dissociation rate of N-CDs to SF were also acquired. This investigation method, with its simplicity, speed, and cost-effectiveness, indicated promising potential application future research into fluorescence nanoparticle-protein interactions.

SF was purchased from Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co and stored at 2–8 °C. 3-Mercaptopropionic acid (3-MPA) was purchased from Shanghai McLean Biochemical Technology Co and stored at room temperature away from light. 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino)-propyl) carbodiimide (EDC) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemistry Science and Technology Co, Ltd with a purity of 98 %, and stored at −20 ℃. N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) was also purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Science and Technology Co with a purity of 99 % and stored at 2-8℃. N-CDs were prepared in our laboratory using the hydrothermal method and stored dry at room temperature. The synthesis and characterization of N-CDs were described in our previous work [46]. All other chemicals were analytical reagent grade. All solutions were prepared with ultrapure water (18.4 MΩ/cm, Millipore Corp.). Exactly 0.1 mol/L phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.4) and 0.3 mol/L citrate buffer (pH 2.7) were prepared.

The schematic diagram of the FM-SPR system was shown in Fig. 1 , which comprised a wavelength-modulated SPR integrated with a fluorescence microscope. Broad-spectrum white light was emitted from an LED light source (wavelength range 400–700 nm, HLV-24SW-3W, Japan) and transmitted through a cage-type coaxial parallel structure. The cage-type coaxial parallel structure consisted of four double-glued achromatic lenses (Len1, Len2, Len3, Len4, f = 30 mm, GCT-L010650, China) and an adjustable diaphragm (range 1–12 mm, GCT-570101, China). This setup was used to position and secure the elements, thereby modulating the dispersed broad-spectrum white light into a quasi-parallel P-polarized beam. The spot size could be adjusted by the adjustable diaphragm according to meet specific requirements. A lifting device (GCM-531402, China) located beneath the cage-type coaxial parallel structure enabled adjustment of the incident light to the proper angle. The glass substrate (K9, n = 1.51, China) side of the FM-SPR sensor chip was coupled to the right-angled side of the isosceles right-angled prism (K9, n = 1.51, GCL-03010, China) using a refractive index matching liquid (n = 1.51, Japan). A cell with a thickness of 0.13 mm and a volume of 150 μL (SiO2, China) was attached to the gold-plated side of the FM-SPR sensor chip using a 0.5 mm thick Press-to-Seal™ silicone sheet (P24745, USA). The prism was placed and secured within the fixtures to prevent leakage. The right-angle prism, FM-SPR sensor chip, and cell were then assembled, and their positions could be adjusted by a screw jack (PHA-110, China). The injection system utilized an injection pump (LSP01-1A, China) and a bubble trap (DIL-0115153, UK) to eliminate small amounts of gas that may enter during sample exchange. The light from the opposite side of the prism was focused and coupled into the optical fiber using the Len5 (f = 19 mm, GCL-010201, China). The reflected light information was captured in real-time by a spectrograph (AH-ISW301, China), and the light intensity was converted into an electrical signal by a photomultiplier tube (H11903-20, Japan). The data were collected and sent to a computer in real-time using the NI data acquisition card (NI PCI-6259, USA). All fluorescence images were collected by a 3 W LED light source and UV filter under 355 nm excitation by a 10x objective (NA = 0.25) and transferred to a computer via a camera (MC50-S, China). The equipment operation, and the processing were facilitated using NI LabVIEW software. The FM-SPR instrument combined the advantages of SPR and FM to successfully monitor the kinetic process of N-CDs labeled SF in real-time and in situ. The setup of the FM-SPR system was showed in Fig. S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of FM-SPR sensor chip substrate
ce:section.ce:para: The silica glass substrate was initially cleaned and then subjected to ultrasonic cleaning with anhydrous ethanol, a 50 % v/v ethanol solution, and ultrapure water for 10 min, respectively. The substrate was then put into UV light cleaner for 30 min to eliminate organic contaminants. Afterward, the cleaned silica glass substrate was treated with plasma for 10 min to improve the adhesion of the metal to the glass substrate [47]. Finally, a 2 nm chromium layer and a 48 nm gold layer were evaporated onto the glass surface of the substrate, respectively. The gold film chip was submerged in the ultrapure water and stored at 4℃ for future use. The gold film mainly consisted of (111) planes and some (222) planes (Fig. S2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of FM-SPR sensor chip surface
ce:section.ce:para: The process of surface functionalization of the FM-SPR sensor chip was illustrated in Fig. 2 . To monitor the real-time N-CDs labeling SF, we immobilized SF on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip using 3-MPA. In step 1, the FM-SPR sensor chip was immersed in the 30 mM 3-MPA solution for 12 h. Due to the formation of the Au-S bond between the sulfhydryl groups present in 3-MPA and the gold film of the FM-SPR sensor chip, which allowed 3-MPA to form a self-assembled layer on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip. The coverage of 3-MPA on the FM-SPR sensor chip was estimated to be approximately 425 ng/cm2 using the method described in previous work [48]. In step2, a mixture of 50 mg/mL NHS and 50 mg/mL EDC was added for 20–30 min to activate the carboxyl group at the end of 3-MPA attached to the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip through the Au-S bond in the step1, and in step3, excess SF solution (50 mg/mL) was added to allow SF to be attached to the FM-SPR sensor chip through the amide bond. In step2 and step3, the chemical reaction involved was the formation of amide bond between the carboxyl group at the end of 3-MPA and the amino group in SF through dehydration condensation reaction, the specific reaction process was as follows: Firstly, EDC would react with the carboxyl group at the end of 3-MPA to form O-acylurea intermediate, but O-acylurea intermediate would be hydrolyzed very easily, so it was necessary to add NHS to react with O-acylurea intermediate to form a more stable NHS-ester, and then reacted with the amino group present in SF to form amide bond, so as to make SF coupled to the surface of FM-SPR sensor chip. Similarly, 50 mg/mL NHS/EDC and a series of concentrations of N-CDs were added in step4 and step5, respectively, to also use NHS/EDC to activate the carboxyl group in SF to react with the amino group on the surface of N-CDs to form an amide bond, so that the N-CDs were reacted with SF thus reaching the purpose of N-CDs labeling SF. Finally, unbound material on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip was rinsed off using PBS buffer. A detailed description of the chemical reactions was shown in Fig. S3.

Using the Scanasyst in air and intermittent-contact mode of the atomic force microscope (AFM, Multimode 8, Bruker) under 0.797 Hz scan rate to observe the surface thickness, average roughness, and morphological changes after the modification of the FM-SPR sensor chip. The 3D images were processed and obtained by NanoScopeAnalysis 2.0 software. The AFM probe mode was SCANASYST-AIR, and the material was the Silicon Tip on Nitride Lever (Bruker). Using manual measurement mode of the contact angle (CA, CSCDIC-200S) to examine the alterations in surface hydrophilicity following the modification of the FM-SPR sensor chip. During the measurement, the FM-SPR sensor chip was placed on the sample stage, then 2 μL of ultrapure water was dropped on the FM-SPR sensor chip, the status of the water droplet on the FM-SPR sensor chip was quickly recorded using the accompanying camera, and finally the water contact angle was measured using the accompanying software. FT-IR (ATR) (wavenumber scale 400–4000 cm−1, INVENIO s, Bruker) was used to observe the changes in the positions of the infrared characteristic absorption peaks following the surface modification of the FM-SPR sensor chip. The data were averaged over 64 scans.
The above three characterization means were used to judge whether the FM-SPR sensor chip was successfully prepared from microscopic and macroscopic perspectives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Binding process of N-CDs and SF
ce:section.ce:para: The surface-modified FM-SPR sensor chip was inserted in the system, and N-CDs solution of 0.5 μg/mL, 1 μg/mL, 2.5 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, and 100 μg/mL was sequentially flowed through the system at a rate of 200 μl/min. The baseline was stabilized using PBS buffer after each pass through a concentration of N-CDs. The SPR signals and fluorescence images on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip were recorded in real-time by the FM-SPR system. All experiments were conducted under the room temperature.

The SPR signal data was collected using the NI LabVIEW software. The incident light intensity (I0) reflected light intensity (IR) and SPR peaks were described by the equation log (IR/I0). The natural logarithm (log (IR/I0)) of PBS without N-CDs was used as a reference and fixed at the point of the resonance peak with the steepest slope to obtain the SPR signal that varied according to the concentration of N-CDs [43,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time monitoring of FM-SPR sensor chip surface modification processes
ce:section.ce:para: To identify N-CDs from self-assembled monolayers on the FM-SPR sensor chip, real-time SPR signal changes were recorded for each modification step. All solution flow rates were maintained at 200 μL/min. As described in Fig. 3 , the SPR signal of the blank FM-SPR sensor chip remained close to 0 from 0 to 300, coinciding with the SPR peak at 616 nm (Fig. 4 a). After 300 s, a 30 mM 3-MPA solution was continuously flowed to generate a self-assembled monolayer on the FM-SPR sensor chip. At this point, the SPR signal increased to approximately 0.02, resulting in the redshift of the resonance peak to 621 nm. After the reaction had lasted for 6000 s, PBS buffer was added to wash away the unbound 3-MPA on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip. Subsequently, the signal decreased to about 0.01, corresponding to the expected resonance peak at 620 nm. At 7100 s, a mixture of 50 mg/mL NHS and 50 mg/mL EDC was added to activate the carboxyl group at the end of the 3-MPA, resulting in a signal increased to approximately 0.067. Additionally, the resonance wavelength shifted to the red end of the spectrum, measuring 624 nm. The reaction lasted for half an hour, PBS buffer was added to wash out the excess NHS and EDC, and the resonance peak was reduced to 621 nm. At 10,768 s, 5 mg/mL of SF solution was added to bind 3-MPA, resulting in a signal increase to nearly 0.078. At this point, the resonance wavelength had shifted to 625 nm, appearing red-shifted. The reaction was continued for half an hour, and the unbound SF was removed by the addition of PBS buffer, reducing the signal to approximately 0.051 and the corresponding resonance wavelength to 623 nm. At 14,230 s, a mixture of 50 mg/mL NHS and 50 mg/mL EDC was added to activate the carboxyl group in SF, and the signal increased to nearly 0.089. The corresponding resonance wavelength was red shifted to 625 nm. The reaction lasted for half an hour, and the excess NHS and EDC were removed by washing with PBS buffer, and the SPR signal was reduced to nearly 0.059. The resonance wavelength was reverted to 623 nm. The position of the peaks in the SPR spectrum for different modification steps were shown in Fig. 4b.

The FT-IR (ATR) spectra of the blank FM-SPR sensor chip, the FM-SPR sensor chip combined with 3-MPA, and the FM-SPR sensor chip combined with 3-MPA and SF were depicted in Fig. S4. The black curve represents the spectrogram of a blank FM-SPR sensor chip, showing only the peak for CO2 in the air. An absorption peak near 1699 cm−1 was observed in the spectrum of the FM-SPR sensor chip covered with 3-MPA (red curve), corresponding to the characteristic peak of the carboxyl group in 3-MPA. The most characteristic peak for SF was located at 1700–1500 cm−1, with 1700–1600 cm−1 assigned to amide I, 1600–1500 cm−1 to amide II, and 1210–1280 cm−1 to amide III [40]. The peaks at 1618.8 cm−1, 1582 cm−1, and 1239.7 cm−1 indicate the assembly of SF on the FM-SPR sensor chip.

Changes in the surface properties of a bare FM-SPR sensor chip, an FM-SPR sensor chip incorporating 3-MPA, and an FM-SPR sensor chip incorporating 3-MPA and SF were investigated using AFM and CA, respectively. After immersing the blank FM-SPR sensor chip in 3-MPA solution for 12 h, the surface monolayer was observed from 6 nm (Fig. 5 a) to 30 nm (Fig. 5b) and the roughness increased from 1.1 nm to 2.28 nm using AFM. From the CA test results, it was observed that the CA value on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip decreased from 92.911° to 26.07° (Fig. 5d, e). This decrease was attributed to the increase in hydrophilic carboxylate groups resulting from the binding of 3-MPA on the surface of the blank FM-SPR sensor chip. After immersing the FM-SPR sensor chip in SF solution using NHS/EDC activated 3-MPA, the growth of the surface monolayer to 80 nm was monitored from the AFM results (Fig. 5c), and the corresponding roughness increased to 16 nm. The corresponding CA value was also further reduced to 8.509° (Fig. 5f). This reduction may be attributed to the presence of more hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl and hydroxyl on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip, indicating the binding of SF. All these results confirm the successful assembly of 3-MPA and SF on a blank FM-SPR sensor chip.

The surface-modified FM-SPR sensor chip was placed into the system. N-CDs solutions of 0.5 μg/mL, 1 μg/mL, 2.5 μg/mL, 5 μg/mL, 10 μg/mL, 25 μg/mL, 50 μg/mL, and 100 μg/mL were sequentially flowed at a rate of 200 μL/min. After each N-CDs concentration was passed, the baseline was stabilized using PBS buffer. The reaction process was recorded in real-time by the SPR signal (Fig. 6 a). The calibration curve of N-CDs concentration versus SPR peak was shown in Fig. 6b. The SPR signals showed an excellent linear relationship with the N-CDs concentration, with a linear coefficient of 0.99913. The linear detection range was 2.5 μg/mL–100 μg/mL, and the real lowest detection limit reached 0.5 μg/mL.

We obtained fluorescence images after labeling SF at various concentrations of N-CDs, as shown in Fig. 7 . The N-CDs bind to the SF on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip, a distinct green fluorescence image with high brightness and contrast was observed. As the concentration of N-CDs increased, the number of green fluorescence points in the same observation area also increased. To confirm that the fluorescence points on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip were caused by N-CDs labeled with SF, control experiments were included in this thesis. As described in Fig. S5a, the N-CDs solution was directly flowed over the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip without any surface modification at a rate of 200 μL/min, and no fluorescence points were captured by the FM-SPR system. Additionally, to demonstrate the green fluorescence points on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip were not caused by the substances added during the modification step, we also collected the fluorescence images after each modification step. As shown in Fig. S5b–d, no fluorescence points were observed on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip after each modification step. This confirmed that the green fluorescence points on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip were indeed a result of the successful labeling of SF with N-CDs. In addition to the above control experiments, we also flowed 25 μg/mL and 50 μg/mL solutions of N-CDs over the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip modified with 3-MPA and already activated by NHS/EDC and did not find any green fluorescence points (Fig. S5e-f). This could further prove that the green fluorescence points found in Fig. 7 were indeed caused by the binding of N-CDs with SF.

The kinetic data for the interaction between N-CDs and SF could be estimated from the change in intensity over time, as shown in Fig. 8 , using Eq. (1). Calculations from the figure indicate that the rate of association of N-CDs to SF was 2.65 × 10−5/s. Using a buffer to separate the N-CDs from the SF, the SPR signal returned to the baseline position, and the resulting dissociation rate was 1.52 × 10−5/s.
(1)
d
I R
/
I 0
dt

We present a novel method for real-time monitoring of the kinetic processes and interactions of N-CDs labeled SF using the FM-SPR system. This was achieved by activating the carboxyl group of SF on the FM-SPR sensor chip with NHS/EDC and binding it to the amino group on the surface of the N-CDs. The results indicated that the fluorescence image on the surface of the FM-SPR sensor chip corresponded well with the SPR signal. As low as 0.5 μg/mL of N-CDs concentration could be detected for labeling SF using this method. It had a wide linear detection range of 2.5 μg/mL–100 μg/mL with a linearity of 0.99913. The experiments demonstrated that the preparation process for the FM-SPR sensor chip was simple and rapid and allowed for real-time and in situ monitoring of the kinetic profile of N-CDs labeled SF and the fluorescence image on the FM-SPR sensor chip surface. This method had potential applications in the fields of protein labeling and cell imaging. Actually, the FM-SPR system also suffered from some shortcomings. According to our observation, compared with the LSCI-SPR system, the FM-SPR system was slightly lower than the LSCI-SPR system in terms of fluorescence imaging quality, such as fluorescence excitation intensity and image contrast, which was a sacrifice that had to be made in order to reduce the cost of the LSCI-SPR system. However, this did not affect the contribution of the FM-SPR system to the low-cost investigation of the interactions between CDs and labeled molecules.

Hongxin Tan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yi Zhao: Validation. Xiuqin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration. Haitao Ma: Software, Resources. Hongyan Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Plan of China (Grant No. 2022YFB3805804, 2022YFB3805802), the Natural Science Foundation of Beijing (Grant No. 4182069), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61378044, 60978034), the Classified Development of Municipal Colleges and Universities-the Project of Constructing the Emerging Interdisciplinary Platform Based on “Clothing Science” of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology (Grant No. 11000024T000003073871) and the Graduate Student Research and Innovation Program of Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology (Grant No. X2023-045).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124417.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
